results.possibleIdentity: John Stafford was a technical collaborator and engineer associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. An engineer at Bell Telephone Laboratories (Bell Labs), he was part of the extensive team of engineers and scientists who provided technical assistance to artists through E.A.T.’s programs. Most notably, he is credited as a member of the technical staff for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, a landmark project organized by E.A.T. that involved the collaboration of dozens of artists and engineers to create an immersive audio-visual environment. His work within E.A.T. focused on the practical application of engineering principles to facilitate artistic experimentation, bridging the gap between scientific innovation and creative expression.

J. N. Stafford (John Stafford) was a British designer and artist active in the 1960s and 1970s, best known for his modern furniture designs produced by Stafford Furniture Ltd. He received significant recognition during this period, including Design Centre Awards from the Council of Industrial Design (CoID) for works such as his 'occasional chair' and 'Flamingo easy chair.' His designs were featured in several major international exhibitions of British design in the late 1960s, including exhibitions in Tokyo and Zurich. While primarily recognized as a designer, his work reflected the period's emphasis on merging aesthetic form with industrial technology and modern materials, a theme often explored by collaborators within the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement.
